title: The Marginal Value of Cash: Structural Estimates from a Model with Financing and Agency Frictions
abstract: How much value does an additional dollar of cash create for a firm? It is generally recognized that the marginal value of cash (MVC) can either exceed or fall below one dollar. Estimates of MVC can guide corporate cash and payout policy, indicate the quality of governance, and make a firm a target for takeover or activism. Yet the existing methods of estimation lack a rigorous theoretical foundation and often provide implausibly high or low estimates. In this paper, we provide a formulation of MVC and structurally estimate the MVC based on a model that encompasses the important determinants for the choice of cash savings, including financing and agency costs. We find that firms with large cash and capital stocks have lower marginal value of cash, whereas the relationship between leverage and marginal value of cash is hump shaped. Two quasi-natural experiments validate the MVC estimates.

author.Sudipto Dasgupta: Department of Finance, Chinese University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong
author.Di Li: HSBC Business School, Peking University, Shenzhen City, Guangdong 518055, China
author.Erica X. N. Li: Cheung Kong Graduate School of Business, Beijing 100738, China

article.1. Introduction: In a seminal paper,Blanchard et al. (1994)examined what firms do with cash windfalls and documented the associated shareholder wealth effects. For a small sample of 11 firms with Tobin’sQwell below unity, they found that managers, instead of returning the cash to shareholders, used it in ways more consistent with the pursuit of private benefits. The median shareholder value gain was 30% of the windfall amount, indicative of shareholder wealth destruction. Ever since theBlanchard et al. (1994)study, financial economists have been interested in the concept of themarginal value of cash(henceforth, MVC) as a way to understand whether firms are putting corporate cash to good use. More cash is clearly valuable; however, exactly how much an additional dollar of cash is worth depends on what firms intend to do with it. For example, financially constrained firms might be able to increase their investment in positive net present value projects with more cash, so an additional dollar of cash is likely to add more to shareholder value for such firms. In poorly governed firms, managers are likely to appropriate some of the value via the pursuit of private benefits (as in the case of a majority of the firms inBlanchard et al. 1994), and the MVC would be low. Empirical estimates of the MVC often imply very large deviations from unity. In one of the first papers to estimate MVC,Faulkender and Wang (2006)estimated an MVC of 0.94 for their sample; however, subsample estimates range from 0.45 to 1.15.1Subsequent studies have used estimates of the MVC to assess the real importance of various (unobserved) frictions and the resulting inefficiencies (or misallocation of resources).2These studies also often show large deviations ranging from as high as 1.77, implying that an additional dollar of cash creates as much as 77 cents of additional value for the average firm, to as low as 0.78, implying a value loss of 22 cents per additional dollar. How plausible are these estimates? Consider the case of estimates of MVC in excess of unity. For a firm that is already raising some external financing, the main reason why the MVC could exceed 1 is that an additional dollar of cash allows it to save the deadweight financing cost associated with the last dollar of external financing. However, this implies that theupper boundof MVC is one plus the deadweight cost of raising an additional dollar of external finance. For the average firm, this deadweight cost cannot be 70%, as some of the MVC estimates would suggest. In fact, structural estimations of models typically imply the deadweight costs of issuance to be around 4%, that is, 4 cents to a dollar, which would suggest an upper bound MVC of 1.04 for firms that raise some external financing.3For firms that do not raise external financing but pay out cash, either in the form of dividends or repurchases, arguably, the MVC is even lower—equal to the value of a dollar in the hand of the investor (otherwise, the value-maximizing firm would retain that dollar instead of paying it out). Agency problems would reduce the MVC, and structural estimations can give the extent of value loss associated with the average firm because of managerial agency problems. In this paper, we construct a model of a firm’s investment, debt financing, and cash holdings decisions in the spirit ofGamba and Triantis (2008),Nikolov and Whited (2014), andGao et al. (2021). The model assumes that managers derive private benefits from diverting a fraction of current cash holdings and cash flows. However, their compensation is tied to firm value and a bonus related to current profit. In this setting, for each realization of a productivity shock, managers choose the stock of capital, financing, and cash holdings to maximize their discounted payoff. We first estimate the model parameters via the simulated method of moments (henceforth, SMM) and generate the firm’s marginal value of cash as a function of the state variables (capital, cash holdings, debt outstanding, and the productivity shock). Second, we extract the quadruplet of state variables for each firm-year observation in the real data. Third, we compute the MVC for each firm-year observation in the real data based on the model-implied MVC function. Our sample average MVC estimate, gross of loss due to managerial agency problems, is 1.0469, with a standard deviation of 0.0306. Net of loss because of managerial agency problems, MVC, is 1.0344, with a standard deviation of 0.0319. Most of the existing literature is based on two closely related methodologies for estimating the MVC.Fama and French (1998)andFaulkender and Wang (2006), respectively, regress the market value of the firm scaled by the book value of assets (annual excess stock returns) on excess cash holdings scaled by book value of assets (change in cash holdings scaled by the lagged market value of equity). We discuss the latter methodology more extensively because it has been more widely used in recent studies. We address two questions. (i) How do we conceptualize the MVC, and (ii) why do the estimates in the literature differ so much from ours? In general, MVC can be greater than, equal to, or less than 1, depending on external financing costs and managerial agency problems. The manager’s optimization exercise equates his or her marginal benefits from retaining an additional dollar of cash to his or her marginal opportunity costs of doing so. The former is precisely the manager’s share of the increase in the present value of future cash flows because of an additional dollar of cash retained, that is,1/(1+r)·Et[∂Vt+1/∂ct+1], plus the increase in the present value of the manager’s future private benefits. The marginal cost of retaining a dollar is the manager’s foregone share of the best alternative use of a dollar of cash. The latter is either the value of a dollar paid out as dividends or of not replacing a dollar of costly equity financing (if the firm raises equity funds in the current period), which includes the deadweight financing cost per dollar raised. The existence of debt financing, however, reduces the marginal cost of retaining a dollar because of increased collateral values and hence, increased price at which new debt can be issued. From the first-order condition, it is straightforward to show that depending on whether the firm raises equity financing, the MVC is either one or one plus the deadweight cost of a dollar of external finance, adjusted for increased price of debt, then minus the marginal private benefit to the manager per unit of ownership. In what follows, we call this the net MVC. Gross MVC is net MVC plus the manager’s marginal private benefit per unit of ownership. Thus, whereas the net MVC is the marginal value of a dollar of cash to shareholders, the gross MVC or the MVC to the manager guides managerial decisions. As regards the second question, we suggest that the widely used methodology, à laFaulkender and Wang (2006), is in fact not estimating the MVC; rather, it is estimating another useful concept—the marginal value of cashflow. This is so because the control variables in the most widely used regression include, other than change in cash, change in net assets, excluding cash, net external financing, and dividends. The firm’s cash flow is left out. Its inclusion would create a multicollinearity problem; by virtue of the cash flow identity, cash flow plus net external financing equals change in net assets plus change in cash plus dividends. However, with the other components of the cash flow identity held fixed, there is a one-to-one relationship between cash flow and change in cash holdings; a dollar increase in the former implies a dollar increase in the latter. Therefore, the coefficient of the change in cash holdings is the same as that of cash flow when the change in cash holding is substituted by cash flow in the set of explanatory variables. We verify this intuition for both our model-generated data and the real data; when we replace change in cash with cash flow, we get coefficients that are identical (for the model-generated data) or very similar in magnitude (for the real data). The marginal value of cash flow is different from the MVC. Even when it can be considered exogenous, cash flow is different from a “helicopter cash drop” (Blanchard et al. 1994). One reason for this is that cash flow is associated with a productivity shock and can contain information about future cash flows because of the persistence of the shock. We verify this intuition in our model-generated data and in the actual data by varying the persistence of the productivity shock. The coefficient of change in cash (alternatively, cash flow) is monotonically increasing in the persistence of the shock. Therefore, we contend that the earlier studies that estimate the MVC may have more relevance for a growing literature on how shock persistence affects firm valuation and corporate policy (Chang et al. 2014,Décamps et al. 2017). In our baseline model, because the estimated model parameters are fixed for every firm in our panel of firms, the only driver of variations in the MVC is each firm’s history of productivity shocks. Thus, the model endogenously generates relationships between firm-specific variables (such as the size, cash holdings, and leverage) and the MVC. We discuss below some of the important relationships that emerge for the imputed MVCs. First, we find that firms with larger capital stock have lower gross MVC, all else being equal. This relationship is consistent with the notion that larger firms are supposed to be less financially constrained in the real economy and hence, have lower MVC because of financing cost. In our baseline model, the cost of external financing per dollar is the same for all firms. Thus, the negative relationship is likely driven by the fact that smaller firms, having lower profits net of fixed operating costs, are more reliant on external financing; therefore, the MVC due to financing cost is on average higher for these firms. Because our model is estimated to match the real economy, the computation of the MVC using the real data is based on the same set of parameters, and similar logic applies for the observed relationship in the real data.4 Second, we find that firms with higher cash holdings or higher increases in cash holdings have lower gross and net MVCs in the model. This is expected because these firms have less need for external financing. Third, we find a hump-reshaped relationship between both gross and net MVCs and the leverage ratio, which is likely driven by the existence of debt capacity and a debt overhang problem. As leverage grows and a firm’s debt level approaches its capacity, the probability of a firm resorting to equity market for financing increases, resulting in a higher marginal value of cash. However, too large of a leverage leads to a debt overhang problem, and part of the marginal benefit of a dollar goes to debt holders instead of equity holders, resulting in lower marginal value of cash. Overall, the gross and net MVCs behave very similarly in terms of their sample distributions and their relations with firm characteristics. The negligible differences between gross and net MVCs are likely due to the small magnitudes of estimated agency cost parameters. We argue that because some of the popular measures of financial constraints are constructed based on firm size, cash holdings, and leverage (among some other firm-specific variables), the above results are consistent with tighter financial constraints being associated with higher MVCs and confirm this via sample partitions based on financial constraints. Finally, to validate our estimation and imputed MVC, we examine two quasi-natural experiments. In our first quasi-natural experiment, we examine the impact of large industry tariff cuts on parameters that capture managerial agency problems and the MVC. We find that managerial diversion decreases significantly by 80%, and bonus parameter almost doubles after tariff cuts. The overall effect on the gross MVC is not significant, but the net MVC decreases significantly, mainly because of higher incentive pay. These findings are consistent with the idea that more intense foreign competition is associated with stronger governance (Dasgupta et al. 2018), and firms provide managers with higher bonuses to compensate them for tougher jobs. This exercise provides validation for our estimation method. The second quasi-natural experiment is the American Jobs Creation Act (ACJA) of 2004, which temporarily reduced the tax on repatriated foreign cash savings from 35% to 5.25% for U.S. corporations.Xu and Kim (2022)identified a group of firms that repatriated foreign earnings under the AJCA in 2004 and after and a group of firms that discussed repatriation of foreign earnings under the AJCA in their 10-K but did not repatriate. We run an ordered logit regression of a firm’s repatriation decision on its MVC, in addition to a set of control variables used in the previous literature (Faulkender and Petersen 2012,Xu and Kim 2022), and find highly significantly positive coefficients for either gross or net MVC. The results are robust whether we use the MVCs in 2003 or the average MVCs in the two- or three-year periods before 2004. This finding indicates that our imputed MVC indeed has a significant predictive power for firm’s decision of earnings repatriation; that is, firms with higher marginal value of cash are more likely to repatriate. We contribute to the empirical literature mentioned above on measuring the marginal value of cash and relating its value with corporate policies and governances. We point out the caveats of these existing MVC measures and provide our own based on structural estimation of a model in the spirit ofGamba and Triantis (2008),Nikolov and Whited (2014), andGao et al. (2021). The most related paper isHalford et al. (2024), who also cast doubt on the existing methodology for estimating MVC. Their main argument is that the existing methodology generates unreasonably large estimates of MVC in some cases, and for firms that should have similar values of MVC, the estimates are drastically different. However, they neither identify what goes wrong with the existing methodology nor provide a correct method to compute MVC, both of which are accomplished in our paper.5 Our paper is also closely related to the theoretical literature on dynamic models of cash holdings (see, e.g.,Riddick and Whited 2009,Anderson and Carverhill 2011, andBolton et al. 2011, among others) and the structural estimation literature (see, e.g.,Hennessy and Whited 2005,2007;Morellec et al. 2012;Taylor 2013;Eisfeldt and Muir 2016;Bazdresch et al. 2018; andLi et al. 2018, among others). We add to this literature by providing a methodology to construct endogenous unobserved variables for a specific firm-year observation based on structurally estimated dynamic models and real data.
article.2. Model: We adopt a direct and structural approach to exploring implications of the cash-saving decision on firm value. For that, we construct and estimate a dynamic model. In this model, the firm delegates decision making to a manager. The manager’s utility is not perfectly aligned with that of investors, and internal cash stock brings private benefits to the manager. Each period, the manager makes decisions on investment, financing, and cash saving to maximize his or her own utility. External financing is costly, and cash stock alleviates investment distortion due to financial constraints. Therefore, the value of cash encompasses both agency costs related to cash and value enhancement from mitigating financial constraints.6 2.1. Timeline We study a representative firmithat lives indefinitely but operates in discrete periods in time. Each period, the firm makes decisions on investments, cash holdings, the amount of one-period debt issuance, and dividend payout (equity issuance if payout is negative). In the spirit ofGamba and Triantis (2008)andGao et al. (2021), the firm’s operations and decisions unfold in a typical period following the timeline given below (for simplicity, the subscriptiis omitted): 2.1.1. Shock Realization. At the beginning of periodt, the productivity shock,zt, is realized and observed by the manager. The productivity shock follows an AR(1) stochastic process,zt=(1−ρz)z¯+ρzzt−1+σzεt,wherez¯is the unconditional mean ofzt,ρz∈(0,1)is the persistence coefficient,σz>0is the conditional volatility ofzt, andεtis a standard Gaussian shock. 2.1.2. Liquidation or Continuation. Given the state variables—capital stock, cash stock, debt liability, and productivity shock—the market value of the firm can be assessed, and the manager makes the decision about liquidation or continuation of operation. We assume that the firm is liquidated if its market value falls below zero. Once liquidated, the firm sells all of its capital at a fire sale discount rate,θ∈(0,1), and pays off debt liabilities with cash stock and the proceeds from sales of capital. Because of the protection of limited liability, all unpaid debt beyond the liquidated value of the firm will be waived. Therefore, debt is risky for lenders. 2.1.3. Production. If the firm continues to operate, it employs capitalktto produce output. The operating profit is given byπt=eztktα−fF−fLkt, whereα∈(0,1)captures both market power and decreasing return to scale,fF>0is the fixed operating cost arising from fixed outside opportunity costs for some scarce resources (e.g., managerial labor) used by the firm, andfL>0is the proportional operating cost. 2.1.4. Debt Payoff. The firm then pays off its debt liabilities with cash stock and production profits. The surplus after debt payoff will be carried over to the next stage. When the funds are insufficient, the firm must sell part of its capital to make up the deficit, which is subject to the fire sale discount. Under certain circumstances, for example, selling all of its capital still does not suffice paying off its debt, then the firm will default, and lenders can bring the firm to bankruptcy. Again, the unpaid debt will be waived because of the protection of limited liability. 2.1.5. Investment. The firm accumulates capital through investment:it=kt+1−(1−δ)kt+fst, whereitstands for investment,δ∈(0,1)is the depreciation rate, andfstis the amount of fire sales of capital in this period, which will be discussed in detail later. FollowingAbel and Eberly (1994), we assume that adjustment of capital stock is subject to quadratic costs:CA(it,kt)=a0·I{it≠0}·kt+(aQ/2)·(it/kt)2·kt,wherea0andaQare positive constants, andI{it≠0}is an indicator that equals one if the investment is nonzero and zero otherwise. Note that investment adjustment cost is crucial for the model to match the level and volatility of investment rate in the data. Instead, the fire sale imposes costs on debt and is important for the model to match the leverage ratio. 2.1.6. Cash Savings. The firm also decides how much cash to save in this period. Cash holdings earn a taxable interest in the next period. Letrbe the risk-free interest rate (and also the discount rate); the interest rate for cash holdings is assumed to berc=r−p, where0<p<ris a (negative) spread earned by holding cash. 2.1.7. Financing and Payout. There are three financing sources for the firm’s investment and cash savings: the surplus (if any) from earlier stages of production and debt payoff, risky debt borrowing, and external equity financing. If internal cash flows plus newly borrowed debt are greater than the demand for investment and cash savings, then the surplus is distributed to shareholders in terms of dividends. Otherwise, the firms must finance the deficit with external equity. 2.2. Managerial Incentives The manager is risk neutral. The manager’s employment contract with the firm stipulates his or her compensation. In this paper, we do not explicitly study the optimality of the managerial employment contract but take it as given. This approach allows us to estimate the effects of managerial contract features without compromising model tractability. FollowingNikolov and Whited (2014), the manager’s compensation consists of two parts: a profit-sharing component and an equity component. Specifically, the manager receives a fraction (0<ξ<1) of the firm’s operating profits as bonus. In addition, the firm grants a proportion (0<κ<1) of ownership to the manager (e.g., through restricted stocks or stock options). That is, for every dollar of payout, the manager receivesκdollars. The equity component of the managerial contract helps align the manager’s incentive with that of investors. The larger theκ, the more the manager acts like an outside shareholder. Nevertheless, the manager’s own utility differs from that of outside shareholders. In particular, the manager can divert a fraction (0<s<1) from the firm’s current operating profits and cash stock. There are two types of agency problems associated with the manager’s decisions. First, both the bonus and the diversion from current operating profits result in the manager preferring a larger firm size, which in turn leads to “empire building.” Second, the manager’s diversion from cash stock induces the firm to hoard more cash than necessary. Both of these actions destroy firm value. 2.3. External Financing External funds can be raised through debt and equity issuance. We assume that, in periodt, the firm can issue one-period debt with a face value ofbt+1that is due in the next period. Because the firm may default in periodt+ 1 (i.e., the debt is risky), lenders price the debt according to the present value of the expected debt payment in periodt+ 1, given the capital stock (kt+1) and cash holdings (ct+1) to be carried over to the next period. Following the discussion inSection 2.1, there are three possible scenarios for debt payment in periodt+ 1. (1) The firm liquidates and defaults. That is,Vt+1⩽0, whereVt+1is firm’s market value att+ 1 and defined later, and the liquidation value is insufficient to pay off the debt, that is,LVt+1=[1+rc(1−τ)](1−s)ct+1+θ(1−δ)kt+1<bt+1, whereτ∈(0,1)is the corporate income tax rate. Lenders receive the liquidation valueLVt+1. Let the indicator of this scenario beIt+1L, which equals one if this scenario occurs and zero otherwise. (2) The firm defaults and goes bankrupt. That is,Vt+1>0, but the sum of operating cash flows, cash stock, and fire sale value of capital is insufficient to pay off the debt, that is,DVt+1=(1−τ)(1−ξ−s)πt+1+τδkt+1+[1+rc(1−τ)](1−s)ct+1+θ(1−δ)kt+1<bt+1. Then, lenders receiveDVt+1. Denote the indicator of this scenario asIt+1D. (3) The debt is fully repaid, and lenders receive face value of the debt,bt+1. Therefore, in periodt, the price of this debt is determined as We assume that external equity financing incurs both fixed and linear costs, whereas debt financing incurs zero issuance costs. Letet⩾0stand for the amount of equity financing. The firm paysϕLetdollars of flotation costs, whereϕL∈(0,1). In addition, each time the firm opts for equity financing, it must pay a fixed costϕFkt>0. The total costs of equity financing areϕFkt+ϕLet. 2.4. Objective Function We first specify the firm’s cash flow, which is affected by the manager’s compensation. Before payout or equity financing, the firm’s cash flows are given by The first term inEquation (2)indicates that after the firm generates operating profits, the manager shares a fraction (ξ) as bonus and diverts some (s) for personal consumption. The second term is from the tax deductibility of depreciation. The third term indicates the firm’s cash stock gross of interest but net of managerial diversion. If operating cash flows and cash stock are sufficient to pay off the debt, the surplus is carried over. Otherwise, the firm must incur fire sales of capital stock to cover the deficit, as shown inEquation (3).8The fourth and fifth terms inEquation (2)are capital expenditures and investment adjustment costs.Qtrefers to the proceeds from newly borrowed debt whose face value isbt+1. Lastly, the firm chooses the level of cash savings,ct+1, to be carried over to the next period. The firm adopts straightforward payout and equity financing policies as follows. The firm pays outd˜tif its value is positive and raises new equity to cover the deficit and pays the costs for equity financing ifd˜tis negative. Therefore, the net cash flows distributed to (or received from) shareholders are Given the manager’s compensation structure and his or her private benefits from diversion, his or her instantaneous utility at timet, per unit of ownership, can be specified as Ignoring the firm’s liquidation decision, the manager chooses investment and cash saving in each period to maximize the present value of his or her lifetime utilities per unit of ownership.9For simplicity, we assume a constant discount factor for both the manager and shareholders, followingNikolov and Whited (2014).Equation (5)shows that the manager’s lifetime utility comes from three parts, that is, firm value, bonus, and diversion, and can be written as The firm chooses to liquidate when the present value of net cash flows for shareholders is negative, that is, whenV˜t≤0. Therefore, we can define the manager’s lifetime utility per unit of ownership,Ut, asUt=U˜tifV˜t>0andUt= 0 if otherwise. Firm value,Vt, the manager’s value from bonus per unit of ownership,Bt, and the manager’s value from diversion per unit of ownership,St, respectively, can be defined likewise. 2.5. Firm Policies and the Marginal Value of Cash In this section, we discuss the firm’s decisions on investment, cash savings, and debt financing. Then, we derive the marginal value of cash. Without the loss of generality, we focus on the viable case (i.e.,Vt=V˜t>0so thatUt=U˜t). To illustrate the intuition, we first derive the first-order conditions of the BellmanEquation (7)forinterioroptimal policies(kt+1,ct+1,bt+1). We rewrite the manager’s optimization problem into the equivalent Bellman’s equation given below: The Lagrangian of the firm can be written as The first-order condition w.r.t.ct+1is given by Equation (9)indicates that the optimal cash saving is determined by the equality between the marginal costs of a unit of cash saving today and the two sources of marginal benefits: (a) the marginal value of cash (i.e., the discounted expected utility from a unit of cash tomorrow),1/(1+r)Et[∂Ut+1/∂ct+1], and (b) the marginal value of relaxing the cash nonnegativity constraint,μtc. Both equity and debt financing contribute to the marginal costs of retaining one more dollar of cash. From the equity financing side, if the firm is not raising equity financing, then the marginal cost per unit of ownership is one; if it is raising equity financing, then the marginal cost is one plus the linear financing costϕL. From the debt financing side, retaining one more dollar of cash raises the collateral value of the debt and increases proceeds of debt financing by the amount ofQtc. Because of the existence of fixed equity issuance cost, the above first-order condition analysis applies only if the firm has nonzero payouts or equity issuance. Otherwise, the firm’s optimization problem has corner solutions, in which case the marginal value of cash has to be computed directly as1/(1+r)Et[∂Ut+1/∂ct+1]once the model is estimated and solved, which is the method we use for imputing our sample MVCs. Based onEquation (6), we can decompose the marginal value of cash from the perspective of firmi’s shareholders into three components: Without agency problems, the net MVC of the firm is exactly equal to the marginal opportunity cost of saving an incremental unit of cash (i.e.,(1+ItEϕL)(1−Qtc)). However, because of the presence of agency costs, the net MVC can be significantly lower if the agency costs are large enough. A firm’s net MVC indicates the increase in the firm value because of one dollar of cash windfall, whereas the gross MVC represents the marginal value of cash for the manager per unit of ownership and is useful in understanding the firm’s corporate decisions, which are made by its manager. In other words, the gross MVC is useful in understanding corporate cash policy, whereas the net MVC tells us about the impact of such a policy on shareholders. 2.6. Discussion One might wonder why we do not define MVC directly in terms of∂Vt/∂ct. This is becauseVtis thecum-dividendvalue of equity that includes the contemporaneous dividend (net cash flowdt), whereas the counterpart market capitalization (price) of equity isex-dividend(i.e.,1/(1+r)Et[Vt+1]) at the end of periodtprior to the realization of the productivity shock of the next period,zt+1. Consistent with the marginal value of cash windfall, we define MVC as1/(1+r)Et[∂Vt+1/∂ct+1], that is, given the optimal corporate policies, the marginal change of market capitalization of equity with respect to one incremental unit of cash windfall.12 The characterization of the MVC inEquation (11)is surprisingly simple. If the firm is raising equity financing in the current period, then the gross MVC equals the marginal cost of equity financing net of the effect of the marginal value from the increased debt collateral. If it is not raising equity finance but paying out cash, then its MVC is one minus the marginal value from the increased debt collateral. Note that the MVC is affected only by the firm’scurrentexternal financing requirement. The cash it allocates to the next period equates the marginal opportunity cost of cash in the current period to the present value of the marginal future benefit; the latter reflects future financing needs. Therefore, the MVC is still determined by the marginal opportunity cost of cash in the current period. It is possible that the firm is neither paying out cash nor raising external financing, which accounts for 358 observations in total and 2.35% of the full sample. This happens if the gross MVC, that is,1/(1+r)Et[∂Ut+1/∂ct+1], is between(1+ϕL)(1−Qtc)and1−Qtc. In this case, the manager does not raise additional external finance because it costs more than its marginal value to the manager, nor does the manager pay out any cash because the marginal value of cash exceeds what it is worth if paid out. The firm could also choose zero equity financing and zero payout because of the fixed costs of issuance (or in reality, information asymmetry). In that case, the gross MVC can be larger than(1+ϕL)(1−Qtc). However, the MVC of the manager is not infinity but rather the external financing cost the manager is willing to pay. The latter is the marginal utility from cash scaled by one plus the modified marginal capital adjustment cost, that is,Et[∂Ut+1/∂kt+1]/(1+r)/[1+aQ(1−τ)it/kt/(1−Qtc)].13 The analysis also shows that the cost of equity financing provides an upper bound for the net MVC (subject to the caveat discussed above that is due to the fixed costs of issuance or information asymmetry, the firm may neither raise equity finance nor pay out cash, and MVC can exceed one plus the marginal issuance cost).14This is intuitive; because a firm can always issue securities to raise financing, an additional dollar of cash cannot be worth more than the additional cost of security issuance. The lower bound can be below one because of the presence of agency costs. Discussions on how debt financing, dividend tax, and signaling benefits affect the marginal value of cash are included in Section D of the Online Appendix.
article.3. Data and Estimation: Our data cover the universe of the ExecuComp firms. This sample restriction is due to the requirement of information about managerial ownership and compensation. Following the literature, we exclude all regulated and financial firms (i.e., firms with SIC 4900 − 4999 and 6000 − 6999). We retrieve financial information from the Compustat database and managerial compensation data from the ExecuComp database. To be included in our sample, the main variables (see Section A of the Online Appendix for their detailed definitions) for an observation must have non-missing values for at least two consecutive years. Our final sample has 15,247 observations for 2,076 firms between the years 1992 and 2017. Summary statistics of the main variables used in the estimation are presented inTable 1, which are comparable to those in the literature, particularly withNikolov and Whited (2014). Table 1.Summary Statistics Table 1.Summary Statistics Notes.Summary statistics for the main variables used in the estimation. The sample covers the firm-year observations in the Compustat and ExecuComp databases between the years 1992 and 2017, with non-missing values of the variables reported in this table, which results in 15,247 observations. Detailed variable definitions are given in Section A of the Online Appendix. As explained in the previous section, our MVC measure depends on the model parameters. The model has 18 parameters in total: the unconditional mean, standard deviation, and autocorrelation of the productivity shock (z¯,σz, andρz), the curvature of the production function (α), the fixed and proportional operating costs (fFandfL), the linear and quadratic adjustment cost parameter for investment (a0andaQ), the profit-sharing (bonus) parameter (ξ), the diversion parameter (s), the depreciation rate (δ), the managerial ownership (κ), the corporate income tax rate (τ), the discount rate (r), the interest rate on cash holdings (rc), and the fixed and proportional equity financing costs (ϕFandϕL). We estimate six parameters,{fF,fL,a0,aQ,ξ,s}and calibrate the other 12 parameters separately. The discount rate as the real risk-free rate is set at 0.02, close to the annualized average three-month T-bill rate minus average inflation rate in our sample period.15The depreciation rate is set at0.079/12per month as the estimate inGao et al. (2021). The fixed and linear equity financing costs parameters are set at 0.007 and 0.054, which are the slope and intercept of a regression of the ratio of issuance fees to assets on the ratio of proceeds to assets (Gao et al. 2021). This indicates that on average the firm incurs a proportional financing cost of 5.4 cents when it raises one dollar externally. We followNikolov and Whited (2014)to set the corporate income tax rate at 20% and the managerial ownership at 0.051. The interest rate on cash holdings (rc) is set at 0.0163, which is close to the estimated interest loss inDeAngelo et al. (2011). As for the parameters related to production and the productivity shock,{α,z¯,σz,ρz}, we first estimate the panel of shocks for the firm-year observations in our sample and then calibrate the parameters based on the AR(1) stochastic process of the productivity shock. The detailed procedure for the calibration of the parameters of the productivity shock process is given in Section B of the Online Appendix. Panel A ofTable 2reports the values of the calibrated model parameters. Table 2.Model Parameter Estimates Table 2.Model Parameter Estimates Notes.This table presents the calibrated model parameters in panel A and the estimated ones in panel B.ris the discount rate;τis the corporate income tax rate;δis the depreciation rate;κis the managerial ownership;αis the curvature of the production function;z¯is the unconditional mean of the productivity shock;σis the conditional volatility of the productivity shock;ρis the serial correlation of the productivity shock;θis the fire sale discount rate;rcis the interest rate on cash holding;ϕFandϕLare the fixed and proportional equity financing costs;fFandfLare the fixed and proportional operational costs;a0andaQis the linear and quadratic investment adjustment costs;sis the diversion coefficient;ξis the bonus coefficient. The standard deviations of the parameter estimates are reported in parentheses. Panel C reports the model-implied averages and variances of cash-to-assets ratio (c/(k+c)), investment-to-capital ratio (i/k), profits-to-assets ratio (e/(k+c)), equity financing-to-assets ratio (ef/(k+c)), payout-to-assets ratio ((1−I)d/(k+c)), and debt-to-assets ratio (b/(k+c)) and also reports the averages of Tobin’sq((V−d)/(k+c)). All moments are averaged across 100 simulations. We estimate the remaining six model parameters using the simulated method of moments (SMM), which selects one set of parameters to minimize the distance between the moments simulated from the model and the data counterparts. To generate the simulated moments, the model must be solved repeatedly when SMM navigates over the parameter space. The model does not have an analytical solution, so we solve it numerically. Section C of the Online Appendix provides a detailed description for the numerical method used to solve the model. As a method of structural estimation, SMM’s identification requires picking enough moments that are sensitive to the model parameters to be estimated. To understand the identification of the model parameters, we conduct comparative statics of the model, shown inFigure 1. In each panel of this figure, we solve and simulate the model 100 times. Each time, we change the parameter in question (on the horizontal axis), whereas the other parameters are fixed at their values in the baseline estimation reported inTable 2. The values of the moments in question are the averages of 100 simulated panels with 5,000 firms and 26 periods. Basically, the comparative statics demonstrate the sensitivity of the moments with respect to the model parameters. The variances of leverage ratio and equity financing help identify the fixed operating cost parameter (fF). As the first two panels ofFigure 1show, a higherfFis associated with greater volatility of leverage and equity financing The means of the ratio of cash to assets (c/(k+c)) and profitability (e/(k+c), wheree=[1−(ξ+s)]π) are informative about the proportional operating cost parameter (fL), because, as the third and fourth panels ofFigure 1show, the proportional operating costfLmotivates higher cash hoarding and depresses profitability. The fifth and sixth panels ofFigure 1show that the variances of both profitability and payout ratio increase in the linear investment adjustment cost parameter (a0). Therefore,a0can be identified by including these two variances. Meanwhile, the mean and variance of the investment-to-assets ratio are used to identify the quadratic investment adjustment cost parameter (aQ) because the quadratic investment adjustment cost depresses both the level and variability of the investment ratio, as shown in the seventh and eighth panels ofFigure 1. We include the means of the cash-to-assets ratio (c/(k+c)) and the M/B ratio ((V−d)/(k+c)), which are informative about the diversion parameter (s). As the ninth and 10th panels ofFigure 1show, higher diversion motivates the manager to hoard more cash, but this action harms the firm’s value. Finally, we use the means of leverage ratio and equity financing to identify the bonus parameter (ξ). The last two panels ofFigure 1suggest that a higher compensation based on the short-term profit motivates the manager to raise more external financing (in terms of both debt and equity). This is likely to be driven by the increased reliance on external funds that is due to the overinvestment motive. Section C.1 of the Online Appendix describes the details of the SMM estimation, and the results are presented inTable 2. Panel B ofTable 2shows that all parameter estimates, except that of the proportional operating cost (fL), are statistically different from zero and highly significant. As indicated inEquation (10), the two agency parameters are crucial for the determination of the net MVC: the bonus coefficient (ξ) and the diversion coefficient (s), which gauge the reduction in the marginal value of cash from the gross MVC. Our estimation shows that the estimate of the diversion coefficient is relatively small in magnitude. The estimates suggest that, on average, the manager diverts 1.52 basis points from one dollar of cash flow or cash stock and is awarded about 0.3 cents for each dollar of operating profit generated. These estimates are similar to those estimated byNikolov and Whited (2014). However, they play important roles in the determination of cash stock as well as the MVC. One standard deviation increase in the diversion coefficient (s) is associated with a 2.1 percentage decrease in the M/B ratio. The data moments as well as their counterparts simulated from the model are reported in Panel C ofTable 2. The SMM estimation works reasonably well because it closely matches most of these moments, except for the variances of investment and external financing. It is common for firm characteristics simulated in structurally estimated models to have lower variations than their counterparts observed in the actual data. More importantly, the moments we choose to match are able to identify the estimated parameters, except for the linear operating cost parameterfL, evidenced by the small standard error of each parameter.
article.4. Results: In this section, we discuss the limitations of the existing measure of MVC and propose our own measure. We study the relationship of our measure of MVC with various firm characteristics, compute the marginal value of cash for each firm-year observation based on our measure, and explore the properties of the sample MVCs. 4.1. Common Methodologies for Estimating MVC One of the most commonly used methods for estimating MVC in the literature is proposed byFaulkender and Wang (2006), and is the regression coefficient on changes in cash holdings in the following regression (adapted to our setting): 4.1.1. The Dependent Variable in Regression (12). The correct dependent variable in regression (12) should be the capital gain during periodt, defined asPt/Pt−1, instead of the realized return,Vt/Pt−1, which includes both capital gain and dividend yield. The empirical measure of MVC in regression (12) is analogous to a partial derivative ofVtwith respect toct+1. Assuming that cash holdingct+1is optimally chosen, the first-order condition implies that The fact that the dividend yield,dt/Pt−1, is one of the control variables alleviates this problem but cannot completely solve it. Because the dependent variable is firmi’s return adjusted using the return on one of the 25 Size/BM portfolios to which stockibelongs att, the average dividend yield of benchmark portfolio also has to be included in the set of control variables.17 4.1.2. The Economic Meaning ofγ1. If regression (12) cannot generate the true value of MVC in the data, what does the value ofγ1represent? Notice that the value ofγ1remains the same if we replace the regressorΔct+1/Pt−1of regression (12) byEt/Pt−1, which is the earnings in periodtand corresponds to the earnings before interest and extraordinary items inFaulkender and Wang (2006). This is due to the cash flow identityEt=dt+Δct+1+it. The r.h.s. of this identity is the use of funds, whereas the l.h.s. is the amount of cash flows that firmigenerates in periodt. Thus, with other items of the identity held fixed, there is a one-to-one correspondence between the change in cash holdings and earnings or cash flows. We argue below that the coefficientγ1captures the increase in firm value per unit of increase in cashflow, not cashholdings.18 We demonstrate this by using 100 simulated panels, each with 5,000 firms and 26 years to match the length of our data sample, based on the model solution under the parameterization reported inTable 2. Regression (12) is estimated for each simulated panel, and the coefficients are averaged across the simulations. We present the results in panel A ofTable 3, where the first column conducts the FW regression (12), and the second column replaces the change of cash stock in regression (12) with earnings. As expected, the two coefficients are identical. Table 3.Faulkender and Wang (2006)Regression Table 3.Faulkender and Wang (2006)Regression Notes.Panel A presents the results of regressing the excess stock returnrit−RitBon firm characteristics based on the simulated data from three model specifications, whereritis the return of firmifor periodt, defined asVitPit−1, andRitBis the return on one of the 25 Size/BM portfolios to which stockibelongs att; the baseline and baseline with the persistence of productivity shock changed to 0.0. The average mean and standard deviation (in parentheses) of the true MVC for firm (λf) across the simulated samples are reported at the bottom. All variables are deflated by the lagged market equityPit−1.Δct+1is the change of cash stock in periodt, andEtis earnings generated in periodt. Control variables include investment (it), the net cash flow to shareholders (dt), the change of dividend payout (ΔDt), cash stock at the beginning of periodt(ct), the interaction betweenctandΔct+1, lagged earnings (Et−1), and the market leverage ratio (Lt). These controls are omitted from the table presentation to save space. Both regression coefficients and their standard errors (in parentheses) are averages across 100 simulations, each with 5,000 firms and 30 years. Panel B shows how theFaulkender and Wang (2006)regression depends on the persistence of productivity shock. We estimate the persistence of productivity shock (i.e.,ρ) at the firm level in a rolling window of 10 years, following the method similar to that described in Section B of the Online Appendix. In column 1, we replicate the baseline regression ofFaulkender and Wang (2006). In column 2, we repeat column 1 and include a dummy variableI{Highρ}that equals 1 ifρis greater than the sample median and zero otherwise and the interaction term between change in cash holdings (ΔCash) and this dummy variable. In column 3, we substitute change in net assets and net finance of theFaulkender and Wang (2006)regression by capital expenditure and net finance, respectively, constructed from the balance sheet data items, as inTable 4. Column 4 is the same as column 3, except that we include the dummy variableI{Highρ}and its interaction term with change in cash. Other control variables are the same asTable 4and are omitted from the table presentation. Standard errors are clustered at the firm level and reported in parentheses. **Significance at the 5% level; ***significance at the 1% level. We then verify this intuition using the actual data. The results are presented inTable 4, and the details about the data construction and definitions are provided in Section E of the Online Appendix. Column 1 ofTable 4replicates the baseline regression ofFaulkender and Wang (2006)(i.e., column I of theirTable 2) using our sample. The regressors are based on balance sheet data items. In column 2 ofTable 4, we replace the change in net assets with the implied investment inferred from the cash flow identity, where the other variables in the identity are constructed using the data items from the balance sheet.19We also replace net financing with that constructed using the balance sheet data. These substitutions are needed because the FW regressors do not exactly match the items in the cash flow identity, although they are economically similar.20Column 2 ofTable 4shows that with such substitutions, the coefficient ofΔCashtis very close to that from the FW regression (i.e., column 1 ofTable 4). In columns 3 and 4 ofTable 4, we do the substitutions alternately for the change in net assets and net financing, and the results are similar. These findings provide support for the use of the variables from the cash flow identity in the framework of the FW regression. Table 4.Nonidentification ofFaulkender and Wang (2006)Regression Table 4.Nonidentification ofFaulkender and Wang (2006)Regression Notes.This table demonstrates nonidentification of theFaulkender and Wang (2006)regression approach. The dependent variable is the excess stock return. All independent variables are scaled by the lagged market capitalization and are self-explained, except thatInvstands for investment,NetFinstands for net financing, andDivmeans dividend payout. In column 1, we repeat the baseline regression ofFaulkender and Wang (2006). In column 2, we replace changes in net assets with investment computed from the cash flow identity and net financing with that constructed from the balance sheet data items, labeled asNetFint˜. Columns 3 and 4 alternately replace changes in net assets with the implied investment and net financing with that constructed from the balance sheet data items. Column 5 repeats column 2, except that changes in cash holdings are replaced with cash flow. Details on data construction can be found in Section E of the Online Appendix. Standard errors are clustered at the firm level and reported in parentheses. ***Significance at the 1% level. The key result is in column 5 ofTable 4, where we show the difficulty of identification in the FW approach because of the cash flow identity. We repeat the regression in column 2 ofTable 4, except that change in cash stock is replaced with cash flow. The regression coefficient of cash flow is very close to that ofΔCashtin column 2 ofTable 4.21Therefore, it is problematic to interpret the coefficient ofΔCashtin the FW regression as the MVC. Moreover, this problem cannot be solved by including cash flow as a control variable because of multicollinearity. In summary,γ1in the FW regression (12) should be better interpreted as the increase in firm value per unit of increase in cashflow, controlling for other firm characteristics, rather than the MVC. Note that cash flow (an indicator of productivity) proxies a state variable, whereas the change in cash holdings is a choice variable. If we assume that the level of cash holdings is chosen to maximize firm value, then as shown inEquation (13), changes in cash holdings should lead to zero changes in firm value because of the first-order condition of value maximization.22However, cash flow, representing a productivity shock, would have a nonzero effect on firm value, which is what the coefficientγ1captures. Based on our interpretation,γ1will increase with the persistence of the cash flow shock because changes in firm value reflect the expected changes in all future cash flows, not just the current one. Higher persistence means that the shock will lead to larger changes in future cash flows. In contrast, the MVC has no obvious directional dependence on the persistence of the shock. For example, the MVC could decrease with cash flow persistence if the shock is positive because of relaxed financial constraint but increase if the shock is negative. We first use the simulated data from our model to confirm this conjecture. Specifically, we estimateγ1using the simulated data under two parameterizations,ρz=0.6945(baseline) andρz=0, respectively, whereas the rest of parameters are at their baseline values. Because we can compute the true MVC for the simulated data, we also compare the average MVC withγ1. For the results, we refer back to panel A ofTable 3. We simulate 100 panels, each with 5,000 firms and 26 years, under the aforementioned three specifications. Several observations emerge. First, the value ofγ1increases with the persistence of the productivity shocks. Under the baseline estimation ofρz=0.6945,γ1is 1.855, and it decreases to 1.387 asρzbecomes zero, respectively. Second, the value ofγ1does not equal the true average MVC to shareholders (net MVC) in the sample,λf. In fact, the averageλfis less than 1 under the baseline specification because of agency costs, whereasγ1is greater than 1. Lastly, consistent with our conjecture, the MVC is higher when the productivity shock is less persistent. Note that regressions coefficients inTable 3are highly significant, and theR2is close to 1 because firms are homogenous in the simulated data, and the firm characteristics included in the regression can explain almost all of the variations of firm value in the model. Second, we test the positive relation betweenγ1and cash flow persistence using the actual data, and the results are presented in panel B ofTable 3. We estimate the persistence of productivity shock (i.e.,ρz) at the firm level in a rolling window of 10 years, following the method similar to that described in Section B of the Online Appendix. We add to the baseline regression ofFaulkender and Wang (2006)a dummy variableI{Highρ}that equals one ifρzis greater than the sample median and zero otherwise and the interaction term between the change in cash holdings (ΔCash) and this dummy variable. Column 2 in panel B ofTable 3indicates that the market values of high-ρfirms are significantly more sensitive to change in cash holdings than those of low-ρfirms, with the difference being 0.102 (s.e. = 0.0403). The same result is found when net financing is constructed using the balance sheet data, as shown in Column 4 of panel B ofTable 3. In summary, the evidence inTable 3confirms our argument that the coefficient on changes in cash holdings in regression (12) measures the increase in firm value per $1 increases in cash flow instead of the marginal value of cash. Next, we propose our measure of MVC based on the approach of structural estimation. 4.2. MVC Based on Structural Estimation In this subsection, we impute the MVC for each firm-year observation in the sample given the observed values of its corresponding state variables and explore the properties of the sample MVCs based on our measure. For a parameterized model, we can impute the MVC for any given quadruplets of state variables. Among the quadruplet, we observe capital, cash holdings, and debt outstanding of each firm in every fiscal year. In addition, we impute the firm-year productivity shock based on sales under the assumption of a Cobb-Douglass production function. Therefore, we construct the firm-year MVC in three steps: (1) estimate the model parameters to match the moments of the data sample; (2) construct the mapping between the MVC and the four state variables under the estimated parameters; and (3) for every firm-year observation, impute the MVC corresponding to its quadruplet of state variables based on the mapping in Step (2). Further details are provided in Section F of the Online Appendix. Following the aforementioned three steps, we construct the firm-year MVCs for the sample between 1992 and 2017. Their distributions are plotted as normalized histograms presented inFigure 2. First, gross MVC (λu) has an average value of 1.0469 and a standard deviation of 0.0306.λureflects the firm’s cost of financing, and our estimation indicates an average cost of 4.9 cents per dollar financing in the sample. Second, the average deductions in the marginal value of cash that are due to bonus and diversion are 0.0075 and 0.0050, which are relatively small compared with the cost of per dollar external financing. However, the distributions ofλbandλshave long right tails, indicating that agency conflicts are severe for some firms. Lastly, the net MVC (λf) has a distribution similar to that of the gross MVC, with mean and standard deviation being 1.0344 and 0.0319.23 4.2.1. MVC and Firm Characteristics. We regress the gross MVC (λu) and the net MVC (λf), respectively, on the following firm characteristics: log of capital, cash-to-assets ratio, change in cash-to-assets ratio, linear and quadratic terms of debt-to-assets ratio, sales-to-assets ratio, investment-to-capital ratio, market-to-book ratio, equity financing-to-assets ratio, payout-to-assets ratio, and bonus-to-assets ratio. Panel A ofTable 5reports the regression of the gross and net MVCs on the state variables in columns 1 and 3, respectively, and the regression of these MVCs on all firm characteristics in Columns 2 and 4. Because of the small magnitude of estimated agency costs inTable 2, gross and net MVCs exhibit largely similar dependence on firm characteristics. Table 5.Model-Implied Gross and Net MVCs vs. Firm Characteristics Table 5.Model-Implied Gross and Net MVCs vs. Firm Characteristics Notes.Panel A presents the regressions of the model-implied gross MVCs (λu) and net MVCs (λf) on a set of firm characteristics. The model-implied MVCs are imputed based on the baseline model solution and the observations on firm’s cash holding, capital, debt outstanding, and sales (to get productivity shocks), which are the state variables of the model. Independent variables EF, PO, and BN stand for equity financing, payout, and bonus, respectively, and the rest are self-explained. Details on data construction can be found in Section E of the Online Appendix. All independent variables are lagged by one period. Panel B reports the average gross MVCs and net MVCs in the top and bottom one-third subsamples partitioned based on financial constraint indicators such as Kaplan-Zingales (KZ), the Whited-Wu (WW), and the Hadlock-Pierce (HP) indices. Standard errors are clustered at the firm level and reported in parentheses. *Statistical significance at the 10% level; **statistical significance at the 5% level; ***statistical significance at the 1% level. Several patterns emerge from panel A ofTable 5. First, consistent with our intuition, larger firms, firms with more cash holdings and those with larger cash increases, have significantly lower marginal value of cash. These firms have more internal funds and are expected to raise costly external financing less frequently. Thus cash is less valuable to them. In general, the negative effect of change in cash on MVCs is larger in magnitude and more significant than that of cash holdings. Both cash and change in cash have stronger and more significant effects on the gross MVC than on the net MVC. Second, gross and net MVCs exhibit hump-shaped relation with debt-to-assets ratio, indicated by the significantly positive coefficient on the debt-to-assets ratio and the significantly negative coefficient on the quadratic term of the ratio. As a firm’s debt level goes up, the firm has less financial slack, and the probability of incurring equity financing becomes larger, resulting in larger MVCs. However, as debt level continues to grow, the effect of debt overhang (Myers 1977) starts to dominate, and an additional dollar of cash contributes more to debt value rather than to equity value, resulting in lower MVCs. Third, productivity does not have an important impact for MVCs, indicated by the insignificant coefficients on sales-to-assets ratio in all the regressions.24Fourth, additional firm characteristics, such as investment-to-capital ratio, equity financing-to-assets ratio, payout-to-assets ratio, and bonus-to-assets ratio, are all insignificant. This is not surprising because the MVC, by construction, is a function only of the state variables, although nonlinearities in the relationships and potential correlations between the state variables and some of the other control variables could render some control variables significant. 4.2.2. Financial Constraints. In our setting, the MVC depends only on the state variables, namely, the capital stock, cash holdings, leverage, and the productivity shock. Several of the commonly used measures of financial constraints, such as the Kaplan-Zingales (KZ) (Kaplan and Zingales 1997) Index, the Whited-Wu (WW) (Whited and Wu 2006) Index, and the Hadlock-Pierce (HP) (Hadlock and Pierce 2010) Index, are constructed based on some of these state variables as well as other variables such as the market-to-book ratio and dividend payouts (KZ), indicator of payout and industry sales growth (WW), and firm age (HP). One approach to determining how MVC is related to changes in financial constraints (FC) is to examine∂MVC∂FC=∂MVC∂Z/∂FC∂Z, whereZis one of our state variables. For both KZ and WW Indices, FC is decreasing in firm size (proxied by the state variablek) and in cash over assets (i.e.,∂FC∂Z<0) and increasing in the leverage ratio (∂FC∂Z>0). Because∂MVC∂Z<0for firm size and cash over assets and∂MVC∂Z>0for leverage, it follows that∂MVC∂FC>0.25Similarly, the HP Index is decreasing in firm size (book value of assets) for size less than 8.4 billion (in inflation-adjusted 2004 dollars).26Accordingly, given that MVC is decreasing in firm size, it increases when the HP Index is higher because of change in firm size. In panel B ofTable 5, we partition our sample based on whether a financial constraint index (namely, KZ, WW, and HP) for a firm is in the top third or the bottom third among all firms in a particular year. The univariate results show that both gross and net MVCs are higher for firms classified as more financially constrained according to the WW and HP Indices. However, the KZ Index shows the opposite pattern. Although these results (except for the KZ Index) are consistent with our expectation, it should be noted that there are other drivers of the financial constraint indices (i.e., apart from our state variables) that might affect the sorting, and hence, the subsample average MVCs might not line up with the constraint classifications (which is likely the case with the KZ Index).
article.5. Applications: In this section, we use two natural experiments to validate our measure of the marginal value of cash and our estimation. In the first experiment, we estimate two subsamples (“before” and “after”) for the same set of firms in industries that were subject to major tariff cuts to see whether our estimation can detect differences in agency costs and MVC following exogenous changes in industry competition. In the second experiment, we classify firms affected by the American Jobs Creation Act (AJCA) of 2004 into two groups based on whether they did or did not repatriate foreign earnings. Groups that repatriated foreign earnings are considered to have greater need for cash. We examine whether the MVC estimates for these groups are consistent with their “revealed” need for cash. 5.1. Subsample Estimation: Groups Before and After Tariff Cuts In this subsection, we validate our estimation method and measures of MVC by applying our methodology on subgroups classified based on corporate governance strength. However, we do not form subgroups based on popular measures of governance (such as the entrenchment index, institutional ownership, and block holding) because such subgroup classifications are likely to be associated with cross-sectional heterogeneity in other model parameters that we have to hold fixed in our estimations. To mitigate this problem, we use tariff cuts as exogenous shocks to a firm’s external governance strength and examine whether our estimated agency cost parameters are able to reflect these changes. We expect that heterogeneity in model parameters that we do not estimate is a less problematic issue when we examine within-firm effects of tariff cuts over a short period of time.27 Dasgupta et al. (2018)examined the effects of competition shocks induced by major industry-level tariff cuts and found that tariff cuts were followed by forced turnovers in poorly governed firms. Tightening of disciplinary mechanisms should result in lower diversion. Therefore, we expect the parametersand the estimatedλsto be lower following industry tariff cuts in the affected industries. The implications regarding the bonus parameterξare somewhat ambiguous. If managers are being overcompensated via bonus payments, we would expect the bonus parameter to decrease after tariff cuts. However, bonus payments can also be part of incentive pay.Dasgupta et al. (2018)found that for retained managers in good governance firms, incentive pay increased following tariff cuts. We combine our sample with the data fromDasgupta et al. (2018)to identify the treated firms as follows. An event year for a four-digit (SIC) industry is defined as the year during which at least one major tariff cut happens for the given industry.28An event window includes three years before and three years after the event year. If there are multiple major tariff cuts within less than three years in the same industry, we merge those events into one event and make the first event year as the event year for these serial events and extend the post-event period to three years after the last event. We conduct separate estimations for two groups of firms: firms in the subsample before the event time (213 observations) and firms in the subsample after the event time (409 observations).29Given the small number of observations, we estimate the diversion parametersand the bonus parameterξonly. For the rest of the parameters, managerial ownership uses the subsample average, and the others take the values from the full sample estimation.Table 6reports the estimated parameter values for these two subgroups and statistics of the imputed marginal values. Table 6.Subsample Estimates: With and Without Tariff Cuts Table 6.Subsample Estimates: With and Without Tariff Cuts Notes.We identify two groups of observations for each major tariff cut from 1993 to 2005; firm-year observations during a three-year period without any tariff cuts before the event are classified as “Before tariff cuts,” and firm-year observations during a three-year period after the event are classified as “After tariff cuts.” This table presents estimated diversion parametersand bonus parameterξfor two groups of observations. The means and standard deviations of the managerial ownership, the gross MVC (λu), deductions in MVC that are due to bonus (λb) and diversion (λs), and net MVC (λf) are also reported. The MVCs of these firm-year observations are imputed based on the estimated parameter values. The last two columns of the table report the differences in the parameter estimates, ownership, MVCs and the correspondingt-stats between these two groups. Several observations emerge. First, the estimated diversion parametersand the bonus parameterξin both subgroups are positive and highly significant. Second, there is an 80% drop in the magnitude of diversion parametersafter tariff cuts, from 0.1174 ‰ to 0.0243 ‰, and the difference is highly significant, with at-value of −2.96. In terms of the loss in MVC that is due to diversion,λsdrops by 75%, from 0.0020 to 0.0005, and the change is highly significant, with at-value of −39.38. These results indicate significant reduction in agency costs that is due to managerial diversion after tariff cuts. Third, the magnitude of the bonus parameterξalmost doubles after tariff cuts, from 0.6008% to 1.1248%, and the change is highly significant, witht= 3.29. As a result, the loss in MVC that is due to bonus,λb, doubles from 0.0063 to 0.0116, and the change is highly significant, witht= 4.23. Overall, the gross MVC does not show significant changes after tariff cuts; however, because of higher bonuses, the net MVC drops significantly, by 0.0048, with at-value of −2.05. Taken together, the results from this subsample analysis confirm that our parameter estimates and the imputed marginal values of cash are largely consistent with the economic mechanisms that they intend to capture. 5.2. MVC and the Decision to Repatriate Foreign Earnings Under the AJCA If our model-implied MVC measure indeed captures how much firms value cash, then it should be able to predict firms’ corporate cash policies. We test this hypothesis by examining firms’ response to the American Jobs Creation Act (AJCA) of 2004. The AJCA in 2004 provided a temporary tax break for firms by lowering the repatriation tax rate from 35% to 5.25% for multinational U.S. firms that had earnings held by foreign subsidiaries. FollowingFaulkender and Petersen (2012)andXu and Kim (2022), we conduct an ordered logit regression, where the dependent variable takes the value of two if a firm repatriated foreign earnings under the AJCA for the 2004–2007 period, the value of one if it discussed repatriation of foreign earnings under the AJCA in its 10-K but did not repatriate, and zero otherwise.30Firms in the first two groups are required to have positive foreign earnings during the previous three years before 2004. In addition to the independent variables in table A.11 ofXu and Kim (2022),31we add to the regression the lagged gross or net MVC in 2003, their average values in the prior two years (2002–2003), or their average values in the prior three years (2001–2003).32 The regression results are reported inTable 7. Consistent with the findings in previous studies, firms with higher unrepatriated foreign earnings and larger tax breaks generated by the AJCA are more likely to repatriate for the 2004–2007 period.33More importantly, firms with higher gross or net MVCs are significantly more likely to repatriate. The coefficient on lagged gross MVC in 2003 is 7.903 witht= 3.287, 9.833 (t= 3.790) for the average gross MVC in the prior two years (2002–2003), and 12.076 (t= 3.931) for the average gross MVC in the prior three years (2001–2003). The coefficients are similar in magnitude when we replace the gross MVC with the net MVC, which is likely due to the small agency costs implied by our estimation. This result provides strong support that our imputed MVCs correctly reflect the value of cash to firms’ managers who make corporate decisions. Table 7.Decision to Repatriate Foreign Earnings Under the AJCA Table 7.Decision to Repatriate Foreign Earnings Under the AJCA Notes.The dependent variable takes the value of 2 if a firm repatriated foreign earnings under the American Jobs Creation Act (AJCA) in 2004, the value of 1 if it discussed repatriation of foreign earnings under the AJCA in its 10-K but did not repatriate, and zero otherwise. The independent variables are based on values in 2003 or earlier. Columns 1, 3, and 5 include the gross MVC in 2003, the average gross MVCs in 2002–2003, and the average gross MVC in 2001–2003, respectively, as an independent variable. Columns 2, 4, and 6 report the similar specifications with the net MVC. Standard errors appear in parentheses. *Statistical significance at the 10% level; **statistical significance at the 5% level; ***statistical significance at the 1% level.
article.6. Conclusion: Companies can accumulate cash for both “good” reasons and “not so good” reasons. Managers, and not shareholders directly, make decisions on cash policies, and so self-serving managers can build up cash to pursue private benefits. On the other hand, precautionary cash savings can be valuable for shareholders. A lack of clarity on this issue has often made companies targets of hostile takeover attempts and shareholder activism, which in turn may have distorted corporate policy. The marginal value of cash, that is, the additional value that the last dollar of cash created for shareholders, should be a good indicator of whether cash is being put to good use. It is precisely this concept that has stimulated a great deal of research on estimating the marginal value of cash, in particular the extent to which it deviates from the benchmark of $1 based on the quality of corporate governance, or financial constraints. One troubling feature of the methods used to generate these estimates is that they are not well grounded in theory. They also often generate very large deviations from the $1 benchmark. In this paper, we first show why existing methods fail to correctly estimate the marginal value of cash. Next, based on a model related to those in the literature that allow managerial agency problems to affect corporate policy (Gamba and Triantis 2008,Nikolov and Whited 2014,Gao et al. 2021), we formalize the concept of the marginal value of cash or MVC (both gross of and net of managerial private benefits). We structurally estimate the model parameters, which allows us to estimate the MVC in the real data. Our estimates of MVC imply much smaller deviations from the $1 benchmark but are nonetheless highly informative in terms of how the MVC is related to corporate policy outcomes such as the stock of cash holdings or capital (i.e., firm size) and the debt ratio.
